data.title: South Korea Sees Rise Of Crypto-Funded Fake Document Market With University Degrees And Legal Records Sold Online
data.description:
A few messages on Telegram are now enough to order a university certificate that looks real enough to pass casual inspection, delivered in hours and paid for in crypto.

In South Korea, investigators are uncovering an expanding underground market where fake official documents are openly traded through social media channels.

Telegram Sales Pitch For Fake Degrees And Certificates

A forger contacted a reporter via Telegram and offered what they claimed were identical copies of official academic documents from top universities, including Yonsei University.

The agent said,

“I can send you a certificate of enrolment [at a top university] for $100. Just provide your information, and we will deliver it to you within two hours.”

Fake Yonsei University graduation certificate being traded on Telegram.

Prices vary depending on the document, with graduation certificates reportedly costing just over $200 in cryptocurrency, while foreign university certificates can reach about $341.

Yonsei University is among South Korea’s most prestigious institutions and ranked 50th in the QS 2026 global rankings.

Another listing showed a local enrolment certificate priced at 150,000 won, with instructions to submit personal details such as name, date of birth, school and grade.

Why Are Crypto And Gift Cards Preferred For Payment

The operators behind these services repeatedly push customers towards digital payment methods, particularly crypto and gift certificates, claiming they help avoid detection.

The forger warned,

“If you pay [for forged certificates] using a bank transfer, you, as the customer, could also become a target of a police investigation.”

They also attempt to build trust by warning potential buyers about other “scam” vendors, while continuing to pressure them into completing transactions.

Inside A Network Selling “Identical” Documents In Hours

The forgery groups advertise a wide range of documents beyond academic certificates, including driving licences, family registration papers, criminal records, bank loan documents and even pension-related certificates.

Some services claim they can recreate any document if provided with a photo or file, delivering results as PDFs or physical copies.

In some cases, delivery is restricted to face-to-face handover, with sellers saying courier services are avoided due to legal risks.

Search terms like “forgery of national technical qualifications” and “fake graduation certificates” are trending in real-time on X.

Prices are structured and negotiated.

Domestic university certificates reportedly start around 300,000 won, while overseas versions can reach 500,000 won. Financial documents cost more due to higher sensitivity.

One seller said, “That is the minimum unit price,” when asked to reduce costs, while also warning buyers that cheaper alternatives could be scams.

Are These Networks Becoming More Organised

Police and investigators say these operations are increasingly structured, functioning like a full-service industry with separate roles for advertising, consultation, production and delivery.

A police official noted that many cases only surface through individual complaints, making it difficult to map the wider system.

An official from the National Police Agency said,

“The structure of these cases involves securing only victim statements in the initial stages and handing the case over to the regional agency, so the overall method of the crime is not clearly revealed at the scene.”

He added that fragmented investigations make it difficult to dismantle the wider network.

Rising Forgery Cases And A Growing Digital Market

Official data shows forgery-related arrests in South Korea rose sharply from 453 in 2021 to 817 in 2023, before easing to 672 in 2024. Foreign suspects numbered 50 in 2023 and 34 in 2024.

(Translated image)

Authorities believe the true scale is larger than reported, as forged documents are often used in wider fraud schemes such as impersonation and scam operations.

How International Students Are Being Drawn In

Universities are also seeing risks emerge within international student communities, where fake language test scores and admission documents are increasingly discussed online.

Warnings have appeared in student forums about forged Test of Proficiency in Korean TOPIK results, with some cases already leading to expulsion and deportation after verification checks.

Students report being targeted through social media ads and messaging apps such as WeChat, where sellers pose as senior students to build trust.

아니 실트보다가 졸업증명서위조 이런게 왜 필요한데...
갑자기 이상한 사람들이 동시다발적으로 게시글 뿜어내고 있는 거 같은디 pic.twitter.com/oykxe9PMfs

— 푸르팅팅 (@greentingting_) April 16, 2026

One international student in Seoul said:

“I am very easily exposed to advertisements for such illegal activities.”

Why Experts Say The System Is Hard To Break

Legal experts argue that the issue goes beyond individual offenders, pointing instead to a wider broker ecosystem.

Forgery can trigger multiple offences if documents are used for admissions or visa processes, including obstruction of official duties or fraud-related charges.

For foreign nationals, consequences may extend to deportation and entry bans.

Professor Hwang Seok-jin of Dongguk University’s Graduate School of Information Security said enforcement gaps remain a core problem.

“The problem is not the individual, but the broker network surrounding them.”

He added that fragmented responses across institutions make prevention harder.

He also warned that unless universities, te sting bodies and immigration systems improve shared verification processes, forged documents will continue to circulate through the same channels.

data.title: When "blue-chip" DeFi projects run into problems, who will foot the bill?
data.description:
Author: Route 2 FI Source: blackswans Translation: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Aave has just suffered a major blow, and this is no longer a simple case of "a protocol being hacked."

A cross-chain rsETH attack has left a nine-figure bad debt hole in what should be the safest cryptocurrency market for DeFi.

Now, the entire industry must face a harsh question: When blue-chip DeFi collapses, who will ultimately foot the bill?

When "blue-chip" DeFi has problems, who will pay the price?

Quick Overview of the Event: What exactly happened?

... Kelp's rsETH application based on LayerZero was compromised at the message transmission layer. The attackers tricked the cross-chain bridge into minting and releasing rsETH without any actual collateral. This batch of uncollateralized rsETH was then deposited into Aave V3 as collateral. The attackers used this as collateral to borrow a huge amount of WETH and other assets before disappearing. After the vulnerability was exposed, the rsETH collateral became worthless assets, and the liquidation mechanism could not restore its solvency. Ultimately, Aave found itself in this situation: The user has a WETH deposit liability. The collateral, however, is rsETH, which is no longer recognized by the market. The difference between the two is the bad debt. Why is this a huge blow to DeFi? This is far more complex than a simple "misconfiguration of a cross-chain bridge in a certain protocol". 1. The adage "Trust the code, not the people" has failed. The code strictly executed the instructions; the problem lies in the combination of multiple systems: a cross-chain liquidity staking token (LST) paired with aggressive collateral parameters, connected to a currency market where the risk control team and core contributors had already largely exited. 2. DeFi is infrastructure, not just a regular application. Aave is not an unknown small mining pool, but a core venue where institutional treasuries, funds, whales, and ordinary users deposit ETH to earn yield. When such a platform suddenly experiences massive bad debts, the claim that DeFi has grown into a safe and stable financial infrastructure is refuted. After the vulnerability was exposed, ordinary users who had deposited ETH and stablecoins on Aave urgently withdrew their funds, directly causing the WETH liquidity pool utilization rate to surge to 100%. A 100% utilization rate means there is no remaining liquidity in the pool—even if the account shows deposits are still there, withdrawals are impossible; users can only wait for borrowers to repay or for new funds to flow in. Furthermore, Aave froze rsETH and related trading markets to protect itself, further exacerbating the panic over trapped funds. Bad debts arising from rsETH loans could not be properly liquidated, creating a funding gap in Aave's WETH pool, with no mechanism for automatic liquidity replenishment. Before governance and a bailout plan address the bad debts, the withdrawal limit for some pools depended entirely on the difference between new liquidity inflows and outflows. Even more dangerous was the subsequent chain reaction: users discovered they couldn't withdraw their deposits but still had lending limits. To avoid becoming the last ones to bear the risk, they began maximizing their borrowing, lending out as many stablecoins and ETH as possible. The logic was simple: since they couldn't withdraw their deposits, they would extract value through lending. Depositors went from net lenders to net borrowers overnight. This wave of bank runs exacerbated the already overloaded liquidity pools, instantly draining the remaining marginal liquidity and drastically increasing systemic risk. High leverage coupled with bad debts will make subsequent risk mitigation and capital restructuring even more costly. The utilization rate of the core liquidity pools (WETH, USDC, USDT) continued to approach 100%, with billions of dollars in assets unable to be withdrawn, and the small amount of newly inflowing funds being instantly drained. The crisis of confidence spread rapidly: Whales and institutions withdrew $5-8 billion from Aave. The price of AAVE token plummeted. Total value locked (TVL) across the DeFi industry declined as funds withdrew from similar lending and cross-chain protocols (Morpho, Sky, Fluid, Kamino, etc.). Other protocols integrating rsETH or using LayerZero suspended cross-chain operations, froze markets, or tightened parameters, further exacerbating liquidity shortages and bringing cross-protocol capital flows to a near standstill. If institutions and users begin to view "blue-chip DeFi money markets" as structurally high-risk products, the overall cost of funds in DeFi will rise across the board: deposits will decrease, interest rates will rise, collateral requirements will become more stringent, and all products that rely on cross-chain assets and multi-layer LST collateral will continue to be discounted. 3. "Code is Law" Loses to "Governance is Game The attack happened quickly, but the fatal hidden dangers had already been sown in months of governance decisions: Continuously increasing the loan-to-value ratio (LTV) of rsETH Compressing the security buffer Blindly pursuing scale expansion Simultaneous loss of key risk control and governance personnel Aave bad debts: Where exactly is the hole? In short: Some users deposit WETH to earn returns. The attackers use rsETH minted through the vulnerability as collateral to borrow WETH. After the attack, rsETH cannot be liquidated at the original price, creating a shortfall in the protocol's WETH owed to depositors. This shortfall is the bad debt. Essentially:

  • WETH pool insufficient collateral

  • Total depositor claims > Remaining asset value of the system

In the past, when similar situations occurred in DeFi protocols, there were usually three solutions:

  1. Using the Treasury / Token dilution (protocol bears the loss)

  2. Depositor write-down (user bears the loss)

  3. A combination of both, along with the "recovery token" narrative

However, Aave modified its backstop mechanism in the past few years, making the situation more complicated.

Who should foot the bill? The core of the controversy is simple: who is currently guaranteeing Aave's solvency? The classic Aave model: AAVE stakers guarantee the protocol through a security module. When a funding gap occurs, some staked AAVE is reduced and sold to restructure capital. As consideration, stakers receive returns and governance rights. This is the default contract: depositors provide liquidity and earn returns; token stakers bear tail risk and receive risk compensation. The reality has completely changed. With Aave migrating to a new architecture and product: The original security module's reduction mechanism has become largely ineffective. Risk mitigation has shifted to a new "umbrella" structure directly tied to specific assets and markets (such as aWETH). In some of these new structures, risk capital is no longer AAVE pledged by token holders, but rather the depositors' own assets. Therefore, in the affected WETH market, the order of actual loss sharing is closer: Left;">For depositors in the corresponding pool: Losses are borne through liquidity freezes, asset write-downs, or mandatory participation in restructuring.

  • Protocol-level guarantee: Governance decides whether to use the national treasury or design a restructuring plan.

  • AAVE holders: Only suffer losses when the new mechanism actually implements reductions or dilutions, far less directly than in the past.

    • This is a completely different implicit contract. If you deposited ETH into Aave thinking "AAVE stakers will guarantee the losses," you might now find that you are the one who actually guarantees the losses. This discrepancy in perception has severely damaged the credibility of DeFi—the advertised risk model has long since become detached from the actual model due to complex governance evolution.

    Why has this completely rewritten the perception of DeFi risk?

    Why has this completely rewritten the perception of DeFi risk?

    Why has this completely rewritten the perception of DeFi risk?

    Direct Impact of the Attack Within 24 Hours

    • Total Value Locked in DeFi Plunged by Over $10 Billion in 24 Hours

    • Aave's Value Locked in DeFi Evaporated by $6.6 Billion, Setting a New Record for the Largest Single-Day Withdrawal in DeFi History

    • AAVE Tokens Fell from $118 to $90

    • Aave Bad Debts Were Approximately $196 Million (Later Updated to $236 Million)

    • Aave WETH Utilization Reached 100%

data.title: Anthropic's one trillion and DeepSeek's ten billion
data.description:
Author: Lin Wanwan

On April 17, 2026, the AI ​​funding circle was excited again.

A screenshot went viral among investors, showing that Anthropic's implied valuation on secondary markets and derivatives platforms such as Caplight and Ventures quietly crossed a threshold: $1 trillion.

Short-lived, but real, it surpassed OpenAI.

There was no official announcement, no press release, and no statement from CEO Dario Amodei; it was simply the pre-IPO market voting for itself.

Investors got excited watching the revenue curve. Anthropic's annualized revenue jumped from $9 billion at the end of 2025 to $30 billion, a 233% increase in four months. Then they started spreading the word: the AI ​​leader has changed. First, let's clarify one thing. Anthropic's latest official post-money valuation is $380 billion, based on its Series G funding round completed in February 2026. Several venture capital firms subsequently offered $800 billion or more, but Anthropic has not yet accepted. That $1 trillion figure is an implicit number on secondary market platforms. Almost simultaneously, another piece of news came from Hangzhou. DeepSeek is planning its first external funding round since its inception, targeting a valuation of over $10 billion and planning to raise at least $300 million. This is the first time in three years. One company was chased by investors, receiving massive funding until it reached the trillion-dollar mark. The other shut out investors for three years, then chose what it felt was the right time to open the door a crack. Reading these two news items together reveals the same thing: this spring, two of the most important AI companies in two different countries have reached the boundaries of their respective paths. Anthropic's Allies List Let's start with Anthropic. On February 13, 2026, Anthropic completed its Series G funding round, raising $30 billion and valuing the company at $380 billion post-money. The lead investors were Singapore's sovereign wealth fund GIC and hedge fund Coatue, with co-investors including Blackstone, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan Chase, Qatar Investment Authority, Temasek, Nvidia committing up to $10 billion, and Microsoft up to $5 billion. Here's the list: Singapore's sovereign wealth fund, Qatar's sovereign wealth fund, the largest US investment bank, Nvidia, and Microsoft. This is a list of companies taking sides. Global capital is voting with real money: the discourse power in AI should remain in the US, and in the hands of this company. Two months later, the results are in. According to monitoring data from enterprise spending management platform Ramp, in March 2026, a staggering 73% of new funds allocated to companies' first-time purchases of AI services went to Anthropic, while OpenAI's share dropped to 27%. Just 10 weeks earlier, the two companies were evenly matched at 50:50. The core weapon is Claude Code, with annualized revenue exceeding $2.5 billion, more than doubling since the beginning of 2026, and enterprise subscribers quadrupling in number. This turnaround can be understood as follows: OpenAI is building a consumer-facing Disneyland, relying on visitor traffic to generate revenue. Anthropic is building a toll road to the core systems of an enterprise; the toll is much higher than the entrance fee, and once a car is on the road, it rarely changes lanes. Just days after Anthropic announced its overtaking of OpenAI, an internal memo written by Denise Dresser, Chief Revenue Officer of OpenAI, was leaked, accusing Anthropic of using the "gross method" to inflate its revenue by approximately $8 billion. When customers purchase services through platforms such as AWS and Google Cloud, Anthropic includes the entire amount paid by the customer in its revenue, including the portion that needs to be shared with the cloud service provider. If this portion is excluded, Anthropic's true revenue is approximately $22 billion, not exceeding OpenAI's $25 billion. The wording of this document is more like two former colleagues revealing each other's shortcomings. Understanding this memo requires some context. Anthropic's private market valuation is approximately $600 billion, a significant premium over its previous funding round, while OpenAI's secondary market valuation is approximately $765 billion, a discount of about 10% over its previous funding round. The company's former parent company is starting to face pressure in the capital markets; issuing this document is both a move to counter its competitor and to stabilize its own position. Then there's that incongruous number amidst the celebratory atmosphere. Anthropic doesn't expect to be profitable until 2027. With $30 billion in annualized revenue and a $380 billion valuation, each funding round breaks records, but profitability is still a long way off. The higher the valuation, the greater the investor expectations, the faster the cash burn, and the more urgent the next funding round becomes. This cycle, Anthropic cannot break actively; it can only maintain by running fast enough. This is its invisible wall. As for DeepSeek, it kept the entire investment community waiting for three years. Then there's Liang Wenfeng. After R1 became a hit, the entire Chinese investment community was in an uproar. Zhu Xiaohu, the man who had just said he "didn't believe in startups building large models," publicly stated that price was no longer important; the key was participation. Tencent executives went, Alibaba executives went, and various VCs came knocking. Rumors of Alibaba investing $1 billion and then $700 million in a Series C round emerged, one after another, only to be denied one after another. Liang Wenfeng effectively shut out the entire investment community for three years. His reason was summed up in one sentence: "There are no short-term financing plans. Our problem has never been money, but the embargo on high-end chips." Huanfang Quantitative Fund invested 3 billion RMB in DeepSeek's first round of R&D, entirely supported by profits from quantitative private equity. He doesn't lack money; he lacks chips, and financing cannot solve the chip problem. As for why he doesn't accept investment, he has another concern: external investors might interfere with the company's decision-making. Reading about Liang Wenfeng's background reveals a consistent approach. Born in 1985 in Zhanjiang, Guangdong, he graduated from the School of Information and Electronic Engineering at Zhejiang University. After graduation, he didn't look for a job but went straight into quantitative investment. In 2015, he founded Huanfang Quantitative Investment, and in 2019, he invested nearly 200 million yuan to build the computing cluster "Firefly-1," equipped with 1100 GPUs.

Upon the launch of the A100, he was ahead of many companies, becoming one of the first people in the Asia-Pacific region to obtain the chip. In 2021, he invested another 1 billion yuan to build "Firefly II," carrying approximately 10,000 A100 chips. In 2023, he shifted the computing power to large-scale models and founded DeepSeek.

He approaches everything with an engineer's pre-judgment approach: prepare the tools first, then get to work. Refusing funding was one of his tools. But now, this tool is starting to fail. DeepSeek's compensation is not low in absolute terms, but it cannot match the equity incentives and valuation premiums of market giants like ByteDance, Alibaba, and Tencent. Liang Wenfeng has begun pushing for company valuation, clarifying option pricing, and giving the team more certainty. Without external funding, there is no market valuation, and therefore no option value. For a top engineer, working at DeepSeek means you might be changing the world, but you can't produce a shareholding certificate that can be used to calculate wealth. In January 2026, Zhipu went public on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange, followed closely by MiniMax. With its peers' stock options being cashed out, DeepSeek's talent pressure is becoming increasingly real. Another issue is being forced to the forefront: executives at DeepSeek and Huanfang are still discussing whether the company should shift from "primarily focusing on research" to "building a business that generates substantial revenue and ultimately profits." This discussion itself is a crack in the door. This initial funding round targets a valuation exceeding $10 billion, while the company's valuation in 2025 is approximately $3.4 billion. If the funding is completed, the valuation will increase several times over.

A $300 million valuation represents less than 3% dilution. This figure is extremely restrained, like someone placing their hand on the doorknob to check its temperature before gently pushing it open.

Liang Wenfeng used three years of independence to gain the biggest bargaining chip for himself. He opened the door when he was most confident.

Two Civilizations at the AI ​​Poker Table

Putting these two stories together, an underlying theme emerges.

Anthropic's Series G investors include Singapore's GIC, the Qatar Investment Authority, Blackstone, Goldman Sachs, Nvidia, and Microsoft. Behind this list lies a complete logic: the discourse power in AI should remain in the US; "safe and trustworthy" AI is the next infrastructure; every penny invested is a bet on this judgment. DeepSeek's initial funding round saw potential investors including Alibaba, state-owned funds, and other leading domestic institutions. This marks the first time Chinese capital has publicly priced a top AI research institution. The bet is based on a different logic: technological independence, open-source ecosystem, and domestic computing power. These two lists, placed on the same table, represent two civilizations placing their respective bets. In this game, closed-source and open-source represent two choices of power structures. Anthropic, entirely closed-source, relies on corporate trust premiums, generating $211 in monthly active user revenue. Besides its model capabilities, it sells a sense of reassurance from expert endorsement; you don't need to understand it, you just need to trust it. Liang Wenfeng says that open source "is more of a culture than a commercial activity; contributing to open source earns us respect." The former concentrates the definition of "what is good AI" in the hands of a few, while the latter leaves it to global developers to discuss. These are two political stances regarding the future of AI. Both companies face the same problem: when you grow large enough, what proves you're worth the price? Anthropic's answer is revenue growth and enterprise clients, but profitability won't be until 2027, and its former parent company is still nitpicking. DeepSeek's answer is still developing. In the end, there are no judges yet. Anthropic's valuation is approaching a trillion, but profitability is likely not until 2027. How long are the world's most astute sovereign wealth funds and top investment banks willing to wait? AI's history is short enough that no one has witnessed a company of this size achieve a soft landing, nor has anyone witnessed a hard fall. Everyone is groping in the dark, albeit in different ways. DeepSeek's problem is the cost of choice. After funding, external shareholders came in. How long can Liang Wenfeng maintain the independence he has been protecting? Once the door is open, no founder in the world can completely control what comes in. Dario Amodei positions himself as "an explorer searching for a third path between the narrow roads to heaven and the narrow road to hell." Those close to Liang Wenfeng say that AGI is his ultimate goal; money and commercialization are not high priorities. Both men believe they are doing something more important than fundraising. The capital market doesn't believe in faith, it only believes in profit statements. Three years later, or five years later, when we look back at this statement: has the company whose valuation once approached a trillion proven it was worth that price? Has the company that earned respect through three years of independence and then decided to take its first step up stayed true to its original intentions? Neither path is yet complete.

data.title: Scenarios, contradictions, and the ultimate outcome as seen by 16 AI payment practitioners
data.description:
AI payment is no longer just a concept. x402, MPP, Tempo, AP2—over the past year, Coinbase, Stripe, Google, and Visa have built protocol frameworks at different levels. Real on-chain data, real merchant integration, and real model misinterpretations are also beginning to emerge. Last Saturday, Zhiwubuyan organized a closed-door meeting on Agent Payment. Sixteen guests from payment infrastructure, wallet services, major companies' payment businesses, and investment institutions answered four questions in three hours: Where exactly does AI payment occur? How can AI spend money securely? How does this business make money? Where will the competition between major companies and startups lead? The following are the core judgments that emerged from this discussion: The most mature scenario for Agent payments is API calls, with single transactions of $0.01 relying on frequency to sustain volume; There is a fundamental conflict between the uncertainty of AI output and the certainty requirements of the financial industry; this is the most fundamental technical contradiction of Agent payments; The security framework of Agent payments is shifting from identity verification to intent verification; The chargeback mechanism in Agent payments... In scenarios where this fails, three-layer arbitration will become a new paradigm for payment security; The design philosophy of large companies is to distrust agents and only trust transactions; The real bottleneck in agent-based payments is not the payment itself, but the upstream transaction process, which has not yet been rebuilt for agents; Startups play the role of component suppliers for large companies, not end-user service providers.

Host

Hazel Hu

Host of the podcast "Zhi Wu Bu Yan", core contributor to the Chinese Public Goods Foundation GCC, X: withhazelhu; Jike: A careless Yueyue

Ivy Zeng

Host of the podcast "Zhi Wu Bu Yan", exploring real-world use cases of Agentic Payment, focusing on Fintech growth, previously worked in post-investment management at a VC firm, and was responsible for regional growth of 2C products at a new type of bank.

X: IvyLeanIn

Thomas Zheng

Head of Capital Markets at Zhi Wubuyan, with 6+ years of experience as a primary market financing advisor, serving multiple leading projects in the industry and facilitating win-win cooperation within the industry

Real-world scenario—Agent payments are already happening, but the form is different from expectations

API calls are currently the most mature on-chain scenario for Agent payments

Thomas Zheng

Analysis of on-chain data from ClawRouter (an application that uses USDC payment to pay LLM APIs) shows that API call scenarios exhibit high-frequency, small-amount characteristics: as of early April 2026, approximately 1,400 unique addresses generated 530,000 transactions, totaling approximately $28,000. Considering the platform also offers a free model, actual usage is likely underestimated—the free portion generates approximately 1 million API calls per month. Image: ClawRouter Official Website

Image: ClawRouter Official Website

Data from a payment infrastructure startup also shows that since starting to deploy the Agentic Payment native payment layer last September, API calls have accounted for about half of the total.

Credit limit authorization is the basic authorization model for Agent payments. The unexpected success of the A2A (Agent 2 Agent) bonus campaign has driven the innovation and popularization of authorization mechanisms. The core of this authorization model is the credit limit rather than approval: users pre-authorize a credit limit to AI, and within that limit, AI can autonomously access funds without requiring confirmation for each transaction. "Within this limit, AI can access your money without your confirmation." Offline consumption hasn't fully taken off yet; the problem isn't payment, it's the experience. Exploration in the online-offline settlement field has covered 50 million real merchants, with scenarios including booking airline tickets, topping up mobile phone credit, and buying gift cards. However, C-end consumption scenarios still face the dual challenges of cultivating user habits and leaping forward in user experience. Experts and KOLs' Distillation Agents Already Have Mature Business Models. Successful cases have validated this approach: renowned doctors and KOLs distill their professional knowledge and content into agents, which users can use when they cannot meet in person. For example, a self-media practitioner distilled their past content into an app, charging 199 yuan per month, and sales have been excellent—a 15-minute live stream with them costs several thousand or even tens of thousands of yuan, while using their agent version only costs tens to hundreds of yuan. Image: Self-media practitioners distill their past content into apps. Transaction Agents Find PMFs Faster Than Payment Agents. Data in the Crypto field shows that transaction scenarios are currently the concentration of real user demand, and their business model naturally possesses a commission-based attribute. Analogous to the early history of blockchain development, those scenario builders who preemptively deployed merchants and stablecoins when gas fees were high, such as Tron, found it difficult for users to migrate even after gas fees increased. The phenomenon of hundreds of millions of users using Qianwendian milk tea during the Spring Festival sparked discussion: were users using it because of a better experience or because of the 25 yuan subsidy per order? The information density of dialogue-based communication is limited; future C-to-B scenarios may require seamless dialogue through smart glasses, demanding a leap in user experience. Attendees listed scenarios that better address user pain points: Procurement scenarios: Scenarios with strict budget control requiring comparison of multiple suppliers (e.g., Alibaba's AI e-commerce agent - Accio) Complex tasks: Scenarios requiring multi-step coordination, such as wedding planning and travel booking Ticket-buying scenarios: High-time-demand needs such as concert tickets Image: Alibaba's AI E-commerce Agent - Accio From a traffic acquisition perspective, Agent payment is similar to early SEO and short videos—representing new traffic opportunities. Those who initially researched SEO, though starting from humble beginnings, consistently found ways to acquire early traffic from SEO. The significance of the "Jinguyuan Dumpling Restaurant" incident may be comparable to the early days of buying pizza with Bitcoin, which will still be remembered by people many years later.

Jinguyuan Dumpling Restaurant Skill Background Story: "On April 7, 2026, amidst the popularity of OpenClaw, the owner of the dumpling restaurant developed an AI capability module called 'Jinguyuan Dumpling Restaurant SKILL'. This AI skill is designed for AI Agents rather than directly for humans. After installation, the AI ​​assistant can autonomously query menu information, business hours, queuing rules, and even obtain a number online. Due to excessive queues during the Winter Solstice of 2025, the food delivery platform's server mistakenly flagged the store's interface as abnormal and blocked it. The owner hopes to optimize the future queuing experience through AI."

Image: Meituan queue skill at Jinguyuan Dumpling Restaurant

True Agent Payments Haven't Started Yet

From a macro perspective, discussing true Agentic Payments may indeed be premature. We can use a child's development as an analogy: currently, they are like children aged 1 to 5, their income comes from their parents, their disposable income is authorized by their parents, what they buy is decided by their parents, and they haven't yet developed their own intentions.

The consensus among participants is that genuine Agent payments are currently concentrated in productivity scenarios: API calls: Large models or API purchases are needed to improve productivity. Enterprise scenarios: Agents in procurement and finance teams for improving enterprise productivity. Vibe Coding: Scenarios for rapid development of demos or products. Identity and Authorization—The Uncertainty of AI vs. Financial Certainty

Agent payment security requires a four-layer framework: identity, risk control, compliance, and arbitration

Payment security can be broken down into three dimensions: identity, risk control, and compliance. AI payments should also follow this framework, with arbitration added as the fourth layer of protection.

I. Identity Layer: Identity Verification is Shifting to Intent Verification

Issuing ID cards to agents, establishing a credit scoring system (building a 5-dimensional scoring standard based on the agent's professionalism, adoption rate, effectiveness, token price, etc.), and completing identity verification. Establishing a traceable and verifiable decentralized DID identity system through blockchain.

Building on this, traditional payment authentication is shifting towards intent verification in agent scenarios. Intent verification needs to consider whether the agent's payment is reasonable, whether the behavioral process meets the requirements, whether it satisfies the final intent, and whether it complies with compliance requirements.

II. Risk Control Layer: The Uncertainty of AI and the Certainty of Finance are Fundamentally Conflicting

There is a fundamental contradiction here: the uncertainty of AI output conflicts with the financial industry's high requirements for certainty and the cost of trial and error. In real-world scenarios:

- Errors in amount recognition have been exposed (0.01 USDC may be read as 10,000 USDC). The editor also encountered the same situation; AI easily misreads the amount of USDC, reading 0.1 USDC as 10,000 USDC.

This is because the field returns a raw integer, but USDC supports 6-digit precision (USDC microunits), so the displayed result is multiplied by 1,000,000. - This is an issue where consumers are easily misled (food delivery descriptions claiming "it can cure all diseases," leading many consumers to place orders).

Image: AI mistakenly reads 0.1 USDC as 10,000 USDC

Meanwhile, developing supply chain poisoning strategies presents a new challenge for risk control. Since the explosive popularity of OpenAI, for example, poisoning a certain npm package, users may not directly use the package, but the packages they depend on use it. Risk control needs to cover multiple layers such as identity authorization layer (anti-money laundering), model side (drift, illusion), and execution chain (poisoning attack). The design philosophy of major tech companies is to assume all agents are malicious by default. They don't pursue "verifiable agents," but rather "verifiable transaction chains." By introducing a mandate protocol, tasks are broken down, constraints are set, and cross-validation is performed. The anti-fraud architecture encompasses layered data, zero-knowledge proofs, the zero-trust principle, and self-verification mechanisms. III. Compliance Layer: The Semi-Decentralized Lightning Network is a Better Solution for Micropayments Traditional finance and blockchain both face bottlenecks when handling massive concurrency. When designing an agent, it should first be defined as a micropayment. The security of micropayments can be designed in a way that is neither too centralized nor too decentralized. The Lightning Network, dormant for many years, may be reborn in the era of Agentic Payments due to its extremely high theoretical TPS. IV. Arbitration Layer: A Layered Arbitration Mechanism Will Replace the Traditional Chargeback Mechanism The traditional credit card chargeback mechanism in the Visa network is difficult to implement in Agentic Payments, necessitating the establishment of a new layered arbitration mechanism: Layer 1: AI automatically arbitrates clear disputes (double charges, incorrect amounts, undelivered services) Layer 2: AI arbitration panel handles the parts requiring judgment (service quality, authorization boundaries) The third layer: Human participation in arbitrating complex disputes. Business models – securing niches, repricing AI, risk control, and licensing. Startups are currently securing niches "out of passion." Before a business model is proven successful, entrepreneurs honestly say, "Out of passion, securing a position, waiting for the right opportunity"—this is how one API platform entrepreneur describes the current stage. The transaction scenario inherently possesses a take-profit attribute. Analogous to the early development of blockchain, those scenario builders who preemptively deployed merchants and stablecoins when gas fees were high, such as Tron, found it difficult for users to migrate even after fees increased. The business model of the trading scenario in the crypto industry inherently possesses a take-profit attribute. Bill aggregation is key to solving the uneconomical nature of small-amount payments. If using a card for payment, merchants may lose money on transactions under $10. In the Agentic Payment scenario, small payments are common; the solution is to perform bill aggregation to increase the single settlement amount. **Pricing by results is only suitable for quantifiable piecework** Users may only call one API, but the results can vary greatly. How should AI services be priced? Participants believed that pricing by results is only feasible for simple piecework (such as the number of work orders a customer service agent resolves); in uncertain scenarios (such as the quality of leads acquired by a sales agent), it is highly subjective. Pricing by results only holds true for a small number of piecework tasks. Mainstream scenarios will likely remain within the old logic of pay-per-call/subscription until the verifiability of agent output is proven.

Pricing your AI product: Lessons from 400+ companies and 50 unicorns | Madhavan Ramanujam

The key to Vibe Coding's commercialization lies in subscription and usage conversion

The goal is to enable new AI companies or ordinary developers to quickly commercialize products developed through Vibe Coding.

Many independent developers find it easy to create product demos, but developing a viable business model is much more difficult. The key lies in how to convert the cost of each user's large-scale use into a monthly subscription plan, or a subscription plus credit model. Competitive Landscape – The Offensive of Large Companies and the Strategies of Startups Stablecoins are Combating Traditional Card Organizations Before acquiring stablecoin company Bridge, Stripe's valuation fell from a high of $92 billion to below $70 billion. After the acquisition, its valuation quickly returned to the $90 billion range, with its latest funding round priced at $159.1 billion. Its stablecoin back-end clearing and settlement service is priced at 1.5%, far lower than the average rate of 2.8% to 3% for traditional card organizations, and may even drop to 1% in the future. In contrast, the business models of traditional payment companies are very fragile (such as Visa's heavy reliance on transaction fees), while PayPal and others, fearing impact on their core businesses, have hesitated in their stablecoin initiatives and failed to achieve significant scale. For a long time to come, the business model may not involve ordinary end-users using these tools themselves, but rather large companies providing unified encapsulation. Large companies may become customers, and entrepreneurs may become suppliers, piecing together the developed tools and then selling them at higher prices. This trend inevitably increases the degree of centralization in the industry. AI tax is the inevitable form of high-frequency, low-value payments within 3-5 years. Some participants believe that AI taxation will serve as a source of UBI (Universal Basic Income) and unemployment benefits, and high-frequency, low-value AI payments will become the underlying infrastructure. Possible tax collection methods include: Introducing the concept of "AI penetration rate" and collecting taxes progressively based on AI penetration rate; Collecting taxes based on token usage, using it as a tax base similar to value-added tax; The real bottleneck isn't in payment, but upstream—the transaction process hasn't yet rebuilt the Agent system. Payment issues seem solvable through protocols and user wallets. However, the biggest problem is that transactions cannot be completed. All payments require a prior transaction; scenarios like e-commerce or airline ticket purchases cannot be completed through an Agent. Without a transaction Agent, subsequent payments cannot proceed. Breaking Through to the Consumer Market: The Importance of Ground Promotion and the Boundaries of Startups Why is OpenClaw suddenly so popular? In China, it was built on ground promotion, driven by large companies selling cloud services and engaging in such promotions. Just like the early promotion of mobile payments, a key reason why even the elderly could use it was the subsidies offered by ground promotion teams—"You install the app, I'll teach you how to use it, and I'll really give you 50 yuan." However, for startups, many needs may take a long time to be met. An AI payment infrastructure entrepreneur stated that after assessing this, they initially decided not to look for user scenarios. They believe that the cost of user education should not be borne by one or two startups, but by the entire industry. If the industry itself is not viable, then it's meaningless; if it is viable, the costs should be spread out by large companies, who can then enjoy the benefits. Conversely, they focus on abstraction—abstracting all accounts, wallets, and even bridges, chains, and payment networks in the industry, so users don't need to understand them. Once they understand this, they know where the small team's winning edge lies and which costs to avoid. This is perhaps the question all Agent payment participants need to answer at this stage: not "Will Agent payments succeed?", but "Before it succeeds, at which layer are you prepared to stand?" Protocol layer, wallet layer, identity layer, authorization layer, transaction layer, clearing and settlement layer—at each layer, there are bettors and waiting players. Large companies are preparing to swallow the entire chain, while startups are preparing to be pieced together. Those who survive are likely those who neither overestimate their ability to independently support a sector nor underestimate their value at any particular layer.

data.title: Bitcoin Push Lifts This Leader to Global No.1 With 94% Approval
data.description:
El Salvador’s president has emerged as the most popular leader in a 26-country approval survey, while also remaining the world’s most visible state-level Bitcoin advocate.

The result puts Nayib Bukele at 94% approval, a level far above other global heads of state, and raises fresh questions about how crypto policy intersects with political popularity.

🚨 94% APPROVAL!

El Salvador President Nayib Bukele just hit a whopping 94% approval rating!

The best approval rating for any leader on earth. pic.twitter.com/C4JwmqyBq8

— Gunther Eagleman™ (@GuntherEagleman) April 19, 2026

El Salvador Tops Global Approval Rankings While Bitcoin Policy Draws Attention

A global survey tracking approval across 26 world leaders places Bukele comfortably in first position with a 94% rating.

The gap between him and other leaders is wide, signalling unusually strong domestic support at a time when many governments are facing political pressure.

The same leadership profile is also tied to one of the most aggressive national Bitcoin strategies anywhere in the world.

Source: X

Since 2021, El Salvador has recognised Bitcoin as legal tender and has continued to accumulate the asset, buying roughly one BTC per day.

National holdings have now climbed beyond 7,600 BTC.

Despite the attention around this policy, polling suggests it is not the main driver of his approval.

In a CID Gallup survey, only 2.2% of Salvadorans identified Bitcoin as a major failure of his administration, indicating that crypto policy sits far behind other public concerns.

Is Security The Real Reason Behind Bukele’s Popularity Surge

The data points strongly to security as the dominant factor shaping public sentiment.

Bukele’s large-scale crackdown on gangs has significantly changed day-to-day conditions in the country, and this shift is repeatedly highlighted as the main reason for his political standing.

While Bitcoin has become a global talking point for El Salvador, local adoption has been far more limited.

The state-backed Chivo wallet saw early uptake driven by incentives, but usage dropped once those benefits ended, with only a portion of users remaining active.

International institutions, including the IMF, have also pressured El Salvador to reconsider its Bitcoin legal tender framework.

In 2021, the government of El Salvador launched the Chivo wallet. Despite bugs, crashes, and even hacks, you could argue it was a success, just for the wrong reason.

In the first year, 25.7% of Salvadorans reported using Bitcoin for payments. But 61% of Chivo users stopped using… https://t.co/lQrXMxfQgZpic.twitter.com/vbdcljzg3T

— 0xluccky (@0xLuccky) April 14, 2026

However, improved economic indicators, including reported GDP growth of around 4% in late 2025, have eased some of that external pressure.

How Other Leaders Approach Crypto Policy And Approval Ratings

The survey also places other world leaders in a different range of approval and crypto engagement.

South Korea’s Lee Jae-myung holds 63% approval and has positioned crypto as a national policy focus, including plans for spot Bitcoin ETFs and a won-pegged stablecoin by 2026.

Argentina’s Javier Milei sits at 48% approval.

He has promoted deregulation in the crypto sector, but his credibility took a hit after backing the LIBRA meme coin, which later collapsed and led to significant investor losses.

In the United States, Donald Trump records 38% approval.

His administration has taken a more structured approach, including an executive order linked to a strategic Bitcoin reserve built from seized assets, signalling a more formal state-level engagement with digital assets.

World’s leaders approval rating:

🇸🇻 Nayib Bukele: 94%
🇷🇺 Vladimir Putin: 72%
🇮🇳 Narendra Modi: 70%
🇰🇷 Lee Jae-myung: 63%
🇨🇿 Andrej Babiš: 55%
🇨🇦 Mark Carney: 54%
🇯🇵 Sanae Takaichi: 53%
🇦🇷 Javier Milei:48%
🇨🇭 Guy Parmelin: 47%
🇲🇽 Claudia Sheinbaum: 47%
🇵🇱 Donald Tusk: 43%…

— World of Statistics (@stats_feed) April 19, 2026

Across Europe, most leaders remain within traditional regulatory approaches, with several governments supporting tighter oversight through the MiCA framework rather than adopting Bitcoin at a sovereign level.

Does Pro-Crypto Policy Help Political Standing Or Not

The wider pattern from the ranking suggests that pro-crypto positions do not automatically harm political approval, but they also do not appear to be the main driver of voter support.

In Bukele’s case, Bitcoin adoption has not significantly influenced his approval either positively or negatively.

Instead, voters appear far more focused on safety and economic conditions.

Similar patterns appear elsewhere, where crypto policies sit alongside, rather than replace, traditional political priorities.

The contrast highlights a practical reality for governments considering digital asset strategies.

Crypto policy may be politically safe in many cases, but it rarely functions as the core reason for electoral support, even when it attracts global attention.

data.title: Vitalik's Hong Kong Speech: The Future Direction and Long-Term Vision of the Ethereum Protocol
data.description: On the morning of April 20th, Ethereum co-founder Vitalik Buterin delivered a speech at the opening ceremony of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival. Vitalik Buterin comprehensively outlined Ethereum's five-year technology roadmap, clarifying Ethereum's core value proposition, phased technology upgrade plan, and underlying principles for long-term development. In his speech, Vitalik stated that Ethereum has two irreplaceable core values, which are also the underlying support for its role as Web3 infrastructure: First, as a decentralized public bulletin board, it provides a trusted on-chain data publishing and notarization platform for various applications, ensuring the public verifiability and immutability of information publication, as well as the equal right of everyone to publish information. It is the core technical support for scenarios such as privacy protocols and secure electronic voting. Second, as a trusted shared computing carrier, it supports shared digital objects automatically executed by code, covering all categories of scenarios including ERC-20 tokens, NFTs, ENS domains, and DAO organizations, providing decentralized applications (DApps) with autonomous, secure, verifiable, and fair participation capabilities. Vitalik also emphasized that the Web3 applications with the greatest long-term value are all based on a deep integration of on-chain and off-chain technologies, rather than simply replicating traditional applications on-chain. Regarding Ethereum's short-term technical implementation plan for the next one to two years, Vitalik outlined four core upgrade directions: **Continuous Iteration for On-Chain Scaling:** Continuously optimize and improve Gas Limits, promote the implementation of CKEVM, and achieve parallel block processing and Gas repricing through a series of EIP proposals. This will enhance the on-chain data publishing and complex computation capabilities while ensuring network security. **Comprehensive Implementation of Account Abstraction:** Promote the implementation of EIP-8141, the account abstraction proposal, to achieve native support for smart contract wallets on Ethereum. This will be compatible with core functions such as transaction gas fee payment, quantum-resistant signature algorithms, and privacy protocols, significantly expanding the application boundaries of Ethereum. **Early Deployment of Quantum-Resistant Security:** Optimize hash- and lattice-based quantum-resistant signature algorithms to address the potential risks of quantum computing. This will be achieved through EVM. Vectorization upgrades address the pain points of low efficiency and high on-chain resource consumption in existing quantum-resistant signatures, completing the underlying technical preparation for quantum security after Ethereum's implementation; Privacy and Storage Capacity Breakthroughs: Continuously strengthen on-chain privacy support, focus on overcoming the technical difficulties of storage expansion, and address Ethereum's shortcomings in storage capacity. The following is the full text of Vitalik Buterin's speech: Good morning, everyone! Where is the Ethereum protocol headed? I think we've seen many significant changes in the theoretical and ecosystem realms. In the past few years, we've also witnessed many changes outside the Ethereum ecosystem, including all the possibilities brought by artificial intelligence, including the possibility that quantum computing may soon be realized, including advancements in formal verification, cryptography, and zero-knowledge proofs. I think one of the many things we've been doing is rethinking what truly makes sense—what is the point of using this protocol? What characteristics does this protocol have that require a random variable to possess these characteristics? For example, how do we integrate this into the Ethereum protocol we've already established, and what are the uses of Ethereum in our five-year plan? I believe it has two main functions: First, the protocol acts like a public billboard, a place where applications can publish messages, and everyone can see the content and order of these messages. These messages can be anything—transactions, hashes, encrypted data, or many other things. In fact, applications have many opportunities to leverage Ethereum as a carrier for publishing data, while simultaneously interpreting that data through other types of protocols—that is, decrypting and computing it. Second, there's the computational function. Ethereum essentially provides you with shared digital objects controlled by code, in various forms: these can be assets, ERC-20 tokens, NFTs, and their significance extends beyond the theoretical level—ENS is a prime example; it can even represent control over an organization, with DAOs being a representative of this. We can use it to achieve many functions, making both of these core functions extremely valuable. For decentralized applications (DAPIs), self-sovereignty ensures application autonomy, verifiability, and fair participation, bringing all users together. Self-sovereignty essentially means that as a user, you can participate, verify, and ensure your own security, entirely based on your own infrastructure. You don't need to trust any third party to run the protocol, nor do you need to trust any third party outside the protocol, if you don't want to. Therefore, verifiability and verification capabilities are crucial—they ensure the correct operation of the chain, verify that everything happening on the chain conforms to the rules, and also guarantee everyone's right to publish information, allowing anyone to post information on this bulletin board. This is the core: we should view the protocol as a technical module and consider all the applications that this technical foundation can support. The most interesting applications are inevitably products combining on-chain and off-chain elements, including ENS and prediction markets. Prediction markets have on-chain components, namely the tradable assets created for each event; and off-chain components, such as oracles. Furthermore, sometimes the design of prediction markets or order matching also occurs on-chain, involving privacy-related aspects. For example, cryptographic protocols have been researched for decades to simplify or enable secure electronic voting. Many such protocols often rely on public bulletin boards for people to post information. In this scenario, people are posting cryptographic votes, ensuring everyone's participation. Anything related to privacy must include an on-chain portion for publishing data and an off-chain portion for interpreting it. Interpreting this data must be done through a private off-chain protocol. Therefore, we often talk about Layer-2 (L2), and my definition of meaningful L2 is as follows: I believe L2 falls into two categories: meaningful and meaningless. Meaningless L2 simply replicates a protocol and amplifies its throughput by 100 times, ultimately becoming more centralized; while meaningful L2 requires us to first review the application, clarifying what off-chain components are included, what parts are included, and what else is needed besides FRAML, before building it. What does this mean for the protocol? We need to scale data, improve on-chain data publishing capabilities, and refine the peer-to-peer network—this was already reflected in the recent hard fork and included in related updates last year, but we still need to push it further. Hashrate scaling is equally important, as it's part of the Ethereum chain and helps different applications integrate and communicate directly without intermediaries. You can visit the Strong Map organization, where there's a roadmap designed for the next five years. The protocol has two core short-term goals: **first, short-term scaling, continuously and actively increasing the gas limit; second, launching CKIVM, which will allow Ethereum to support more scenarios and perform more complex calculations while still easily verifying on-chain information.** Furthermore, we are advancing early preparations for the post-quantum era—we've been focusing on quantum computing for years, understand the challenges it will bring, and have developed some countermeasures. **In the short term, we will refine the quantum security protocol and optimize the entire roadmap.** Our ultimate goal is to achieve complete quantum security for the protocol, ensuring that all its components are secure and efficient, while improving the "modular" construction process and strengthening privacy support. Therefore, there are many EIP proposals for short-term scaling, which will be implemented in later stages: the block access list after the protocol hard fork can be processed in parallel, and gas repricing can improve both efficiency and security, making higher gas limits safer. EPBS extends the time required to verify the protocol and blocks, further improving security and enhancing the protocol's capabilities—it supports download state EIP-8141, an account abstraction proposal that is simple yet powerful. Essentially, a transaction is a series of calls, one of which may be verification, and another may be execution. This allows the protocol to easily achieve native support for smart contract wallets, enabling payments for others' transactions, and also supports quantum-resistant signature algorithms and privacy protocols. This makes Ethereum more versatile and capable of supporting more functions. Quantum-resistant signature algorithms have existed for 20 years; we understand their principles and know how to construct them. However, their efficiency is low: a quarter-byte persistent signature requires 2000 to 3000 bytes, while current signatures only require 64 bytes; on-chain gas consumption is also as high as 200,000, while current signatures only require 3000. Therefore, we plan to adopt two signature types: hash-based signatures and lattice-based signatures. Our approach is to add vectorization functionality to the EVM, using the same principle as computers rapidly running artificial intelligence logic—in the past, we often processed large amounts of data in parallel; this logic can also be applied to quantum-resistant signatures, striving to make signatures both resistant to quantum attacks and efficient. Account-level storage, account balances, and the extended execution of smart contracts are relatively easy to implement, but storage expansion is more difficult, and we still have much work to do in this area. This is our short-term and long-term plan for Ethereum, and it is also the direction we hope Ethereum will take. Ethereum is not designed to compete with high-frequency trading platforms, and the protocol's goal is not to be the fastest chain, but rather a secure, decentralized chain—a chain that remains online and can be trusted. One of our goals is to maximize secure consensus: if the network is secure, it means it can withstand a 49% node failure, and even continue operating normally when almost all nodes are offline, thus possessing the same security characteristics as Bitcoin. Even if the network experiences problems, we can still maintain 33% security certainty; this is the first part of our goal. The second part of our goal is to formally verify all content. We have already begun actively using artificial intelligence to generate data proofs to verify whether the software running the long-term version of the protocol possesses the required characteristics. We have made some progress, which was completely impossible two years ago. Artificial intelligence is developing rapidly, and we are fully leveraging this advantage while pursuing ultimate simplicity, making the long-term protocol as simple as possible and preparing it for the future as much as possible. Therefore, a protocol needs to pass an "off-field test"—if a protocol is to be deployed in real-world applications, it must be reliable and function even without power (no electrical outlets available). This aligns with Bitcoin's goals and is something we must strive to achieve: to ensure the long-term security of digital assets, we need to build a system that continuously provides security, independent of the continued existence or work of any single team. Lean consensus combines the advantages of two approaches: a Bitcoin-style global chain approach and a BFT (finality) approach, simultaneously possessing optimal security characteristics, quantum security, and fast finality. Therefore, finality can be achieved within 1 to 3 slots, with the chain's finality time expected to be approximately 10 to 20 seconds, or even less. zkVM (Zero-Knowledge Virtual Machine) allows you to verify the correctness of the chain without relying on a mainframe computer to run all operations—everyone should verify the chain before trusting it, even your mobile phone and IoT devices should be able to verify the chain. Currently, zkVM is fast enough to prove the feasibility of real-time virtual machine execution. This year, our goal is to ensure its security and start deploying zkVM on a small scale across the network, then gradually increase its application coverage. By 2028, through the adoption of zkVM, Ethereum will be able to scale to handle more transactions while maintaining its decentralized nature. What is the vision behind all this? Ethereum is a world computer. It is both a global shared layer for making commitments, publishing data, recording actions, and actions that different users want to take; and a platform available to everyone where data can be proven to have been published or not. Simultaneously, it is a global shared layer for ensuring the enforcement of high-value rules—a protocol needs to be highly robust and easily verifiable. I believe that in the future, leveraging the power of artificial intelligence will make ensuring software security simpler and easier than we imagine. If you want to ensure software security, but people are unwilling to prioritize security, then the number of software vulnerabilities and the frequency of attacks will be 10 times greater than before. Therefore, as a blockchain, Ethereum first needs to ensure security, and secondly, decentralization; once these two conditions are guaranteed, we must provide this security to users as much as possible. If you intend to build decentralized applications, you need to ensure their self-sovereign security, verifiability, and the right of users to participate. This covers multiple fields such as finance, decentralized social networking, and identity authentication, including both financial and non-financial applications, including ENS and prediction markets. The core goal of the protocol is to simplify application development, which is also our default direction. The roadmap for the next four years is designed around this goal. Thank you!

data.title: Options: If you don't understand, then the money you deposit is just for the project team's yachts and young models.
data.description:
Author: DD DiDidi; Source: X,@rtk17025

In a bear market, many people choose to put their money into wealth management products, but in the current environment, the collapse of DeFi projects has become commonplace.

And if you don't understand what kind of magic tricks the project teams are playing, you're just meat on their chopping block.

Therefore, this time we want to start from the most basic logic and learn about the underlying structure of DeFi: options.

I. How Humanity First Made a Choice for the Future Imagine turning back time thousands of years to the ancient Middle Eastern desert. The protagonist is Jacob. He travels a long way to his uncle Laban's house and falls in love at first sight with Laban's youngest daughter, Rachel. Jacob desperately wants to marry Rachel, but he is a penniless fugitive and cannot afford the lavish dowry required by society at the time. In a typical cash transaction (cash on delivery), Jacob is not even in a position to discuss the marriage. Moreover, if he spends several years slowly saving money, the beautiful Rachel might have already been betrothed to another wealthy young man. Faced with the enormous risk of an uncertain future, what should Jacob do? He proposed to Laban: "I'm willing to work for you for seven years in exchange for the right to marry Rachel seven years later." Laban agreed. The two thus entered into a contract that defied time and the future. This is actually the four core elements of a choice.

  • Buyer: Jacob. He is the one who wants to control the future.

  • Seller: Laban. He accepts the benefits and promises to fulfill his obligations in the future.

  • Underlying Asset: The right to marry Rachel. In modern times, this could be Bank of America stock, Bitcoin, or gold.

  • Premium: Seven years of free labor. Jacob must pay a price to "buy this right." This is like the premium we pay when buying insurance; once paid, it's gone, but in return, it provides future security.

  • Expiration Date: Seven years later. The contract specifies the exact time for fulfilling the promise.

What problem did Jacob solve through this contract?

He used his current labor (royalty) to lock in a future price and right, eliminating the risk of Rachel marrying someone else within those seven years. This is the most fascinating aspect of options: they empower people to combat the uncertainty brought by time.

The Earliest DeFi Collapse: Counterparty Risk What's interesting about this story is that the latter half also incorporates the earliest DeFi collapse event: the project team secretly replacing the founders. After seven years, Jacob prepared to exercise his rights (to marry Rachel). However, the cunning seller, Laban, reneged on the agreement on the wedding night! He secretly replaced his eldest daughter, Leah, with Rachel in marriage to Jacob. This is counterparty risk, where the other party to the contract fails to honor their promise, causing the contract to be unable to be fulfilled as scheduled. This is also the earliest DeFi collapse. II. How can a contract trade the future? In Jacob's case, he locked in a promise of the future with seven years of labor. The mechanism of modern financial markets is to convert such verbal promises into standardized contracts, which are strings of code in a computer system. As for why a contract can be used to trade the future, and why its price can fluctuate dramatically, we can understand it through the everyday act of placing a home purchase order.

Understanding the Essence of Options Through Home Purchase Deposits

Suppose someone is interested in a 10 million NTD house in the city center. Rumors circulate that a nearby MRT station may be announced next month. If the MRT station is confirmed, the house price could soar to 15 million NTD; if the rumors don't come true, the house price could drop to 8 million NTD.

1. Buying Calls: A Strategy for Expecting a Significant Rise

This is the most intuitive way to go long. When a trader strongly believes that an asset will rise significantly in the future but doesn't want to invest all their capital, they will buy a call. For example, if an asset is expected to rise from its current price of NT$100 to NT$150, a trader can pay a premium of NT$5 to buy a call with a strike price of NT$110. If the price does rise to NT$150, the trader has the right to buy at NT$110, netting a profit of NT$35 after deducting the NT$5 cost. If the price falls below NT$110, the trader abandons the exercise, with a maximum loss of only NT$5. This is a typical example of limited risk and unlimited profit.

2. Buy Put: A strategy for anticipating a market crash or hedging. This is like buying insurance for your assets. Traders buy puts when they anticipate a market crash or want to protect their physical assets. For example, if you hold an asset worth $100 and are worried about a price drop next month, you pay $5 to buy a put with a strike price of $90. If the market crashes and the price drops to $50, you still have the right to sell the asset at $90. The value of the put will surge as the price of the underlying asset falls. This is also a strategy with limited risk and high potential profit. 3. Sell Call: A strategy for anticipating a price decline. This is one of the strategies for earning premiums, typically used in markets where prices are expected to consolidate or decline moderately. The seller receives a premium from the buyer and promises to sell the asset to the buyer at the strike price if the price exceeds the strike price. If a seller sells a call (naked short selling) without holding the underlying asset, they face unlimited risk of loss should the asset price rise indefinitely. Therefore, institutional investors often combine their existing holdings with covered call strategies to generate additional profits during periods of sideways market movement. 4. Selling Puts: A strategy for those who expect no price drop or to establish a position at a target price. This is an often overlooked but widely used strategy by many value investors and quantitative traders. Puts are sold when a price is not expected to fall significantly, or when a trader hopes to buy the asset at a lower target price. For example, if an asset is currently priced at 100 yuan, and a trader feels that 80 yuan is an excellent entry point, they can sell a put with a strike price of 80 yuan and immediately receive a premium. If the price doesn't fall below 80 yuan by expiration, the contract expires, and the premium is earned for free. If the price falls below 80 yuan, the trader is obligated to buy the asset at 80 yuan, which aligns with their original plan to establish a position at 80 yuan. Furthermore, because the premium has already been received, the actual purchase cost will be lower than 80 yuan. These four quadrants form the foundation of all complex derivative financial products. Buyers exchange limited risk for leverage and freedom of choice; sellers bear extreme risk in exchange for a fixed return over time. However, in the real trading world, contract pricing isn't simply about price fluctuations. The value of an option also involves market panic and the passage of time. This leads to the core hurdle in Wall Street and cryptocurrency quantitative models, a hurdle many advanced traders must overcome. V. From Wall Street to the Crypto World: IV. Greeks and the True Core of Options Risk

When options, this sophisticated financial instrument, was moved from the traditional Wall Street trading floor into the year-round, wildly volatile cryptocurrency market, the rules of the game fundamentally changed.

In the traditional stock market, investors might wait an entire quarter for a TSMC earnings report, and market volatility is relatively predictable. But in the cryptocurrency world, a sudden piece of news over the weekend can cause Bitcoin or Ethereum to fluctuate by more than ten percent. In this extreme environment, simply guessing price movements is far from sufficient when conducting quantitative arbitrage or establishing hedging positions.

If you imagine yourself standing in front of a giant blackboard, trying to dissect all the variables affecting contract prices, you will find that the pricing model of options is essentially a set of multi-dimensional calculus equations.

To analyze these variables, financial economists invented a system of indicators called "Greeks." The true starting point of this system is Implied Volatility (IV). Implied Volatility: Pricing Fear and Greed Before understanding Greeks, one must first understand IV. IV is not the historical amplitude of past price movements, but rather the collective consensus among market participants regarding the degree of future volatility. When the market anticipates a major market move (such as a significant upgrade to a Layer 2 network or a Federal Reserve announcement of an interest rate cut), people frantically buy options for speculation or hedging. This buying frenzy drives up the contract price. Substituting this inflated price back into the pricing formula, the calculated value is IV. In simple terms, IV is the fear and greed index of the options market. A higher IV indicates that the market believes the future is more volatile, and the higher the option premium; a lower IV indicates a lower option premium. The first level of risk dashboard includes Delta, Theta, and Vega. With the concept of IV, we can access the options risk control dashboard. The three core indicators correspond to price, time, and volatility, respectively. Delta represents price sensitivity, also known as directional risk. It's defined as how much the price of an option changes when the price of the underlying asset changes by $1. You can think of Delta as a speedometer when driving. If your call option's Delta is 0.5, it means that for every $1 increase in Bitcoin's price, your contract value increases by $0.50. Theta represents time decay, also known as time risk. Options are assets with a shelf life, and Theta measures how much value your contract loses each day, all other things being equal. For the buyer, Theta is like a relentless taxi meter ticking down, deducting money every day, like holding a melting block of ice; but for the seller, Theta is like daily interest. Vega represents volatility sensitivity, also known as emotional risk. It measures how much the contract price will change when the implied volatility (IV) changes by 1%. In the cryptocurrency world, Vega's influence often outweighs Delta's. Sometimes you may have correctly predicted the direction and Bitcoin may have indeed risen, but as market sentiment returns from extreme euphoria to calm, the IV drops sharply, causing losses from Vega to eat up the profits from Delta. This is known on Wall Street as the "Voltage Crush."

Advanced Fine-Tuning Gears: Speed, Color, Ultima

If financial markets were only affected by the three variables mentioned above, quantitative trading would be too simple. The reality is that when market prices change, Delta, Theta, and Vega themselves also change. To address this dynamic change, higher-order Greek letters were developed.

To understand these higher-order letters, we need to first mention Gamma. Gamma is the acceleration of Delta.

It measures how much Delta changes when the asset price changes by $1. Speed ​​is the rate of change of Gamma. In physics, if Delta is velocity and Gamma is acceleration, then Speed ​​is jerk. It measures how quickly Gamma changes as the underlying asset price continues to move. This is crucial for managing very short-term, high-frequency positions with volatile prices. Color measures the effect of time on Gamma. As the contract expiration date approaches, the value of Gamma changes. Color tells traders how their acceleration (Gamma) changes each day. Ultima is the third derivative of volatility. When IV changes, Vega changes, and the metric that measures the rate of change of Vega is called Vomma. Ultima further measures how Vomma changes when IV changes again. These extremely small values ​​are typically used only by institutions managing hundreds of millions of dollars and engaging in extreme arbitrage of the volatility curve.

The Ghosts of Cross-Dimensionality: Vanna and Charm

In modern quantitative research, what truly fascinates advanced traders are the Greek letters representing cross-dimensional interactions, the most famous of which are Vanna and Charm.

... Vanna measures the impact of volatility (IV) changes on Delta. This might sound counterintuitive: why would changes in volatility affect my sensitivity to price direction? Because when the market panics (IV rises), contracts that were previously out of reach and extremely out-of-the-money suddenly become "possible." This expansion of possibility stretches the Delta distribution of the entire portfolio. When extreme market conditions like cascading liquidations occur in the crypto market, Vanna is often the driving force behind market makers frantically buying and selling spot to hedge risk. Charm measures the impact of time on Delta, also known as Delta decay. As time goes by, the chances of a contract with no performance value recovering become increasingly slim. Charm describes how the Delta of this contract gradually approaches zero over time. The True Core of Options Risk: From the basic Delta to the complex Vanna, these letters reveal the ultimate truth of options trading: you are never buying or selling a single-dimensional asset, but a four-dimensional space interwoven with price, time, volatility, and probability. Novices die from misjudging the direction (misreading Delta), veterans die from time (being worn down by Theta), and experts often die from volatility (being devoured by Vega and Vanna). Of course, this article isn't just about teaching people how to hedge risks, but more importantly, it aims to equip everyone with the ability to understand the deceptive practices of DeFi project teams.
